blocks.md: You have just taken over a production Linux server. In front of a critical script, `ls -l` shows `-rwxr-xr--`: nine little characters that single-handedly decide who may read, change or run what. Learning to read them at a glance is the alphabet of Linux security.

On Linux, every file and every directory belongs to an owning **user** and to a **group**. Access rights are organized into **three classes**: the owner (**u**), the group (**g**) and **others (o)**, meaning everyone else on the system. Each class is granted three permissions: **read (r)**, **write (w)** and **execute (x)**, hence the shorthand **rwx**.

blocks.question: Your ls -l shows -rwxr-xr--. Your best guess: what can a random user on the system (neither the owner nor a group member) do with this file?

choices.text: Read it, but not modify or run it
Read it and run it
Nothing at all

blocks.reveal: The nine letters read in blocks of three: owner rwx, group r-x, others r--. For everyone else in the world: read-only.

blocks.md: Let's take it step by step. The `ls -l` command displays these rights as `-rwxr-xr--`. The first character indicates the type (`-` for a file, `d` for a directory). Then come three groups of three letters: the owner's rights, then the group's, then those of others. A dash `-` in place of a letter means "permission not granted". Here: the owner has `rwx`, the group `r-x`, and others `r--`.

blocks.question: A quick bet: in octal notation, r is worth 4, w is 2, x is 1, added up per class. What does chmod 644 do to note.txt?

choices.text: rw-r--r--: only the owner can write
rwxr--r--: the owner can do everything
rw-rw-rw-: everyone can write

blocks.reveal: 6 = 4+2, so rw- for the owner; 4 = r-- for the group and for others alike. Result: rw-r--r--.

blocks.md: That **octal notation** is the compact way of writing rights: each permission is given a value (`r`=4, `w`=2, `x`=1), and the values are added up per class. So `rwx`=7, `r-x`=5, `r--`=4. The **`chmod`** command (change mode) modifies these permissions: `chmod 755 script.sh` gives `rwxr-xr-x`, and `chmod 644 note.txt` gives `rw-r--r--`. The **`chown`** command, for its part, changes the owner and the group.

blocks.statement: On a directory, the x permission is what lets you run the programs stored inside it.
blocks.explanation: On a directory, x grants traversal: entering it and reaching a file by name. Whether a program can run depends on the x bit set on the file itself.

blocks.md: Classic trap: on a **directory**, the `x` bit does not mean "run a program". It grants the right to **traverse** the directory, that is, to enter it and reach a file by name. Without `x` on a directory, even with `r`, you cannot access its contents; with `r` alone, you can list the names but cannot descend into it.

cards.back: The three basic permissions: read (r), write (w) and execute (x).
Rights are defined separately for the owner, the group, and then everyone else.
Command that changes the permissions of a file or directory (e.g. chmod 644).
Numeric way of writing permissions: r=4, w=2, x=1, added up per class.

blocks.md: You run IT for a small company. Half the staff are administrators on their machines, "because it's more convenient". One morning, one of them double-clicks a booby-trapped attachment, and what happens next comes down to a few precise mechanisms: account types, tokens, UAC.

Windows distinguishes two broad types of account. A **standard account** lets you use the machine (office work, browsing) but **not** install software system-wide, change protected settings or touch other users' files. An **administrator account**, a member of the local **Administrators** group, controls the entire system. The right habit is **least privilege**: do your day-to-day work with a standard account.

blocks.question: Signed in with an administrator account, you launch an ordinary application. What rights does it run with by default?

choices.text: Full administrator rights, since the account holds them
Standard rights: the admin privileges are set aside
SYSTEM rights, above even the administrator

blocks.reveal: Counter-intuitive but true: even as an admin, your programs run with standard rights by default, through a filtered token. That is UAC's job.

blocks.md: This is where **UAC (User Account Control)** comes in. Even when you are signed in with an administrator account, your session receives a **filtered token** with standard rights by default: your programs run with limited privileges. When an action requires administrator rights, UAC displays an **elevation prompt**. Technically, an admin account holds a **split token**, that is a full token and a filtered token; elevation switches to the full token. This limits the damage done by malware launched inside your normal session.

blocks.statement: As long as UAC is enabled, no program can gain administrator rights without a prompt appearing on screen.
blocks.explanation: False: known bypasses exist (auto-elevating binaries, DLL hijacking). UAC raises the bar, but it is not a security boundary.

blocks.md: Microsoft in fact states this officially: **UAC is a convenience, not a security boundary**. Known **UAC bypasses** exist: auto-elevating binaries, DLL hijacking, and so on. UAC raises the bar and forces a deliberate click, but determined local malicious code can sometimes elevate without showing a prompt. Never rely on UAC as your only protection.

**UAC is not an absolute security boundary.** It is a safeguard that forces explicit consent and limits unintentional elevations, but bypasses exist. Always combine it with a **standard account** for daily use and the principle of **least privilege**.

cards.back: Account with limited rights, without control over the system as a whole.
Member of the Administrators group; full control of the machine.
Mechanism that runs with standard rights by default and prompts for elevation on sensitive actions.
An admin account holds two tokens: a filtered one (standard) and a full one (admin).
Principle of granting only the rights that are strictly necessary.

blocks.md: In Greek mythology, Kerberos (Cerberus) is the three-headed dog guarding the gates of the underworld. The protocol designed at MIT in the 1980s lives up to its name: three players (the client, the KDC, the resource) and one mission: guarding the entrance to nearly every corporate network running Active Directory.

**Active Directory (AD)** is Microsoft's directory service: it centralizes **users**, **computers**, **groups** and their rights within a **domain**. The servers that host AD are the **domain controllers (DCs)**. Instead of a local account on every machine, a user has a single **domain account** that works everywhere. To prove identity, AD relies primarily on the **Kerberos** protocol.

blocks.question: Your session is open and over the day you access ten servers in the domain. How many times does your password travel across the network?

choices.text: Ten times, encrypted on each connection
Once, at logon
Zero: it never travels; tickets stand in for it

blocks.reveal: Kerberos' founding principle: the client proves it knows the password without ever sending it, then presents tickets.

blocks.md: Kerberos is a **ticket-based** authentication protocol. The central player is the **KDC (Key Distribution Center)**, running on every DC, which comprises two services: the **AS (Authentication Service)** and the **TGS (Ticket Granting Service)**. Key idea: **the password does not travel across the network on every access**. The user authenticates once, receives tickets, and then presents those tickets.

blocks.question: At logon you received a "master ticket": the TGT. Now you want to open a file share: who do you present it to?

choices.text: The file server, directly
The TGS, which exchanges it for a service ticket
The AS, to redo a full authentication

blocks.reveal: A TGT is never used directly against a resource: you exchange it with the TGS for a dedicated service ticket.

blocks.md: The flow, in three steps: 1) At logon, the client proves to the AS that it knows the password (using a key derived from it). The AS returns a **TGT (Ticket Granting Ticket)**, a "master ticket" valid for a limited time (about 10 hours by default) and encrypted with the secret of the special **krbtgt** account (only the KDC can read it). 2) To access a resource (a file share, a server), the client presents its TGT to the TGS and requests a **service ticket**. 3) The client presents that service ticket to the target resource, which validates it. The user therefore proves who they are **without ever resending their password** to each server.

Key exam point: with Kerberos, **the password does not travel on every access**. The user obtains a **TGT** (master ticket) from the **KDC**, then exchanges that TGT for **service tickets** for each resource. It is **tickets** that travel across the network, not the password.

cards.back: Microsoft's directory service, centralizing users, computers and rights within a domain.
Server that hosts Active Directory and authenticates domain accounts.
Kerberos service, hosted on the DC, that issues tickets; it comprises the AS and the TGS.
Master ticket obtained at logon; it is then used to request service tickets.
KDC service that exchanges a valid TGT for a service ticket for one specific resource.

blocks.md: You administer a fleet of 3,000 Windows machines. Monday morning, the order comes down: automatic session lock, SMBv1 disabled and a stronger password policy, everywhere, by Friday. Visiting each machine? Nobody does that. This is what GPOs are for.

A **GPO (Group Policy Object)** is a set of configuration rules that Active Directory pushes **automatically** to the computers and users of a domain. Rather than configuring each machine by hand, an administrator defines a policy once and AD applies it to thousands of endpoints. The scope is very broad: password policy, allowed software, services, firewall, session lock, and more.

blocks.question: Two GPOs contradict each other: the one linked to the domain enforces a setting, the one linked to the machine's OU enforces the opposite. Which one applies?

choices.text: The domain's: the highest level wins
The OU's: the last one applied wins
Neither: the conflict cancels both settings

blocks.reveal: The order of application is Local, Site, Domain, then OU: the policy applied last (the one closest to the object) wins by default.

blocks.md: GPOs are **linked** to AD containers: the **site**, the **domain** or an **OU (Organizational Unit)**. A machine applies the GPOs of every level it belongs to. The order of application is **Local → Site → Domain → OU** (mnemonic: LSDOU); by default, the policy applied **last** (the OU closest to the object) **wins** in case of conflict. Clients refresh policy periodically (roughly every 90 minutes) or on demand with `gpupdate`. The files live in **SYSVOL**, a share replicated to every DC.

blocks.question: To harden those 3,000 machines, where does an experienced security team start?

choices.text: It writes its own rules, starting from a blank page
It applies a recognized baseline (CIS, Microsoft), then tailors it
It installs an antivirus and leaves it at that

blocks.reveal: Nobody reinvents hardening: you start from a proven baseline, adapt it to your context, and test it before rolling it out.

blocks.md: GPOs are a central **hardening** tool: enforcing a strong password and lockout policy, disabling vulnerable protocols (SMBv1, LM/NTLMv1), restricting membership of the local administrators group, configuring the firewall, or deploying **AppLocker** to control which programs are allowed. Rather than starting from scratch, teams apply a recognized **baseline** (Microsoft Security Baseline, CIS Benchmark) and then tailor it.

To harden an estate, don't start from a blank page: apply a recognized **baseline** (Microsoft Security Baseline, CIS Benchmark) via GPO, then test it on a limited scope (a **pilot OU**) before rolling it out broadly. A badly configured GPO can break production as fast as it protects it.

cards.back: Set of configuration rules pushed automatically by AD to machines and users.
Active Directory container that groups objects and to which a GPO can be linked.
Share replicated to every domain controller where GPO files are stored.
Command that forces an immediate refresh of Group Policy on a machine.
Recognized hardened configuration template (CIS, Microsoft) used as a starting point.

blocks.md: 1988: the Morris worm brings a large part of the young Internet to its knees. Among its entry points, a buffer overflow in Unix's `fingerd` service. Nearly forty years later, this class of flaw remains one of the most exploited in the world. Let's see why it is still so common.

A program reserves memory areas called **buffers** to store data, for example text typed by a user. A **buffer overflow** happens when the program writes **more data than the buffer can hold**, without checking the size. The excess spills into **adjacent memory** and **overwrites** whatever was there.

blocks.question: A program writes 200 bytes into a buffer sized for 100, with no checking at all. What is the worst realistic outcome?

choices.text: The program crashes: annoying, nothing more
The extra data simply gets truncated
An attacker takes control of the execution flow

blocks.reveal: The excess overwrites neighboring memory, including control data such as the return address. And that is where everything plays out.

blocks.md: Why is this dangerous? On the **stack**, right next to a buffer, sit **control data**, notably the **return address** (where the processor resumes execution when a function ends). By overflowing, an attacker can **overwrite that return address** and **hijack the execution flow** toward code of their choosing (historically, injected **shellcode**). In short: a buffer overflow **overwrites control data** and can hijack the program. The root cause: unsafe functions and the absence of bounds checking in C/C++ (`strcpy`, `gets`).

Modern systems stack several protections. **DEP / NX (Data Execution Prevention / No-eXecute)** marks **data** pages (such as the stack) **non-executable**: the processor refuses to run code from them, which blocks classic injected shellcode. **ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization)** **randomizes** memory addresses (stack, heap, libraries) on every run, so the attacker cannot predict where to jump. The **stack canary** places a secret value just before the return address; the function checks it before returning and aborts if an overflow has changed it.

blocks.question: DEP makes the stack non-executable, ASLR scrambles the addresses, the canary stands guard. Is the buffer overflow dead and buried?

choices.text: Yes: combined, these protections close the door
No: attackers work around them, notably with ROP

blocks.reveal: They are mitigations, not cures: ROP reuses code that is already executable, and one information leak is enough to defeat ASLR.

blocks.md: These mechanisms are **mitigations**, not definitive cures. Attackers respond with **ROP (Return-Oriented Programming)** to bypass DEP by reusing code that is already executable, or with **information leaks** to defeat ASLR. Combined, these protections greatly raise the cost of an attack, but the real fix remains **safe code**: bounds checking, safe functions and memory-safe languages (Rust, Go).

Match each protection: **DEP/NX** prevents **code execution in data areas** (stack, heap); **ASLR** **randomizes** memory addresses; the **stack canary** detects stack corruption before a function returns. The buffer overflow itself **overwrites control data**, first and foremost the return address.

cards.back: Memory area reserved by a program to temporarily store data.
Writing more data than the buffer can hold, overwriting adjacent memory.
Protection that makes data areas non-executable in order to block injected code.
Randomization of memory addresses on every run to make targets unpredictable.
Secret value placed before the return address to detect a stack overflow.

blocks.md: You are a pentester. Your payload has just run and a shell opens on the target server… as `www-data`, an account with next to no rights. The real work starts now: becoming root.

**Privilege escalation** means moving from limited rights to **higher rights** on a system, typically from a standard user to **root** (Linux) or **SYSTEM / Administrator** (Windows). It is an almost unavoidable stage of an intrusion: an attacker rarely lands with full power; they arrive with a modest account, then **escalate** their privileges.

blocks.question: From Claire's account (an ordinary user), an attacker takes control of Karim's account, another ordinary user. What is this move called?

choices.text: Vertical escalation
Horizontal escalation
Lateral movement

blocks.reveal: Same level of rights, different account: horizontal escalation. Lateral movement, for its part, means hopping from machine to machine (more on that later).

blocks.md: There are two forms. **Vertical** escalation gains **more** rights than the account holds (user → admin/root): this is classic "privesc". **Horizontal** escalation means taking control of **another account at the same level** (for example another user) in order to reach their data or widen the attacker's foothold.

blocks.question: Your best guess: what most often gets an attacker to root in practice?

choices.text: A zero-day flaw in the kernel
Brute-forcing the root password
A misconfiguration: a forgotten SUID binary, an overly broad sudo rule

blocks.reveal: In most cases there is no need for a novel exploit: an overly permissive configuration is more than enough.

blocks.md: The most common paths come from **misconfiguration** rather than a brand-new vulnerability. On **Linux**: a **SUID binary** (which runs with the rights of its owner, often root), an overly permissive **sudo** rule (`sudo -l` lists them), a writable script run by root (cron), or overly broad permissions on a sensitive file. On **Windows**: a service whose executable can be modified by a standard account, an **unquoted service path**, **token impersonation**, or stored credentials. In both cases: an **unpatched** kernel or piece of software targeted by a known local exploit.

Most privilege escalations exploit a **misconfiguration**, not a brand-new vulnerability. Defensive habits: apply **least privilege**, audit **SUID** binaries and **sudo** rules, fix file permissions and **patch** quickly. A simple `sudo -l` or a forgotten SUID binary is often all it takes to become root.

cards.back: Moving from limited rights to higher rights (root, SYSTEM) on a system.
Gaining higher privileges than the account holds (user to admin/root).
Taking control of another account that has the same level of rights.
Special bit that makes a program run with the rights of its owner, often root.
Linux mechanism that lets a user run certain commands with elevated rights.

title: Logging and EDR: detecting on the endpoint

blocks.md: Monday, 8 a.m. You discover that a server in your fleet has been compromised. For three weeks. The investigation's first question: what happened during all that time? The answer depends on a choice made long before the attack.

blocks.question: On this server, logging had never been seriously configured. What can the analyst hope to reconstruct?

choices.text: Most of it: a disk always keeps usable traces
Almost nothing: without logs, no usable evidence
Only the list of stolen files

blocks.reveal: Without logs, an intrusion leaves virtually no usable evidence. Detection is prepared before the attack.

blocks.md: To detect an attack on a machine (an **endpoint**, workstation or server), you first need **traces**. **Logging** records events: logons, process creations, file access, service changes. On **Windows**, this is the **Event Log**, for example event **4624** (successful logon) or **4688** (process creation), enriched by **Sysmon**. On **Linux**, it is **auditd** and **journald/syslog**. Without logs, an intrusion leaves no usable evidence.

Logs tell you **what happened**, but reading them by hand across an entire estate is impossible. An **EDR (Endpoint Detection and Response)** is an **agent** installed on every endpoint that **continuously collects telemetry** (processes, network connections, file and registry changes), **detects suspicious behavior**, and can **respond**: kill a process, **isolate** the machine from the network, roll back changes.

blocks.question: A Word document launches PowerShell, which connects to the internet. No known malicious file is involved. Who can raise the alarm?

choices.text: The classic antivirus, by signature
The EDR, through behavioral analysis
No one: without a known signature, nothing to detect

blocks.reveal: No file is known to be malicious, yet the chain of behaviors is suspicious: that is exactly the EDR's home turf.

blocks.md: How it differs from a classic **antivirus**: traditional antivirus relies mostly on **signatures** of known malicious files. EDR adds **behavioral** detection (a Word document that launches PowerShell, which then reaches out to the internet, forms a suspicious chain even without a known signature) and the ability to **respond**. The telemetry is generally forwarded to a **SIEM** so it can be correlated centrally across the whole estate by the **SOC**.

Remember how they complement each other: **logs** tell you **what happened** (after the fact), while **EDR** adds real-time **behavioral detection** and **response** (isolate the machine, kill the process). One does not replace the other: centralize your logs (SIEM) **and** deploy an EDR.

cards.back: End device on the network: workstation or server, a target for monitoring.
Recording of system events (logons, processes, access) for analysis.
Agent that collects endpoint telemetry, detects suspicious behavior and can respond.
System that centralizes and correlates logs from an entire estate for detection.
Windows tool that enriches logging with detailed events (processes, network).

blocks.md: June 2017: NotPetya paralyzes entire multinationals within hours. Its lightning spread did not owe everything to the EternalBlue exploit: the malware also stole credentials from memory and replayed them from machine to machine via PsExec and WMI. That chapter is called **lateral movement**, and one of its favorite weapons is **pass-the-hash**.

On Windows and Active Directory, the **NTLM** protocol never sends the password across the network. Instead, the system stores and uses an **NTLM hash** derived from the password. During NTLM authentication, the server sends a **challenge** and the client answers it using its NTLM hash (challenge-response mechanism). The consequence: to prove you are a given user, all you need is to **hold their NTLM hash**. The cleartext password is not required.

blocks.question: An attacker gets hold of an administrator's NTLM hash, not the password. What must they do with it to log on as that admin?

choices.text: Crack it first, to recover the cleartext password
Replay it as is: the password is superfluous
Nothing: a hash cannot be used to authenticate

blocks.reveal: With NTLM, the hash is the secret: holding it is enough to answer the challenge-response. That is the whole principle of pass-the-hash.

blocks.md: **Pass-the-hash (PtH)** exploits exactly that: an attacker who has obtained a user's **NTLM hash** can **authenticate as that user without ever knowing their password**. They are not trying to **crack** the hash; they **replay** it directly. These hashes are typically stolen from the memory of the **LSASS** process (with tools such as Mimikatz) on an already compromised machine, or from the local **SAM** database.

blocks.question: The same local administrator password is deployed on all 200 machines of a fleet. The attacker steals the hash from a single machine. How many machines are within reach?

choices.text: Just one: the hash is specific to each machine
All 200: same password, same hash, everywhere
None, as long as they lack the cleartext password

blocks.reveal: The hash derives from the password, not the machine: one theft unlocks the entire fleet. That is exactly what LAPS prevents.

blocks.md: Pass-the-hash is a favored engine for **lateral movement**: once a first machine is compromised, the attacker **hops from host to host** to reach their target (often a domain controller or a sensitive server). If a **local administrator account shares the same password** (and therefore the same hash) across many machines, a single stolen hash unlocks them all. The techniques rely on **SMB / PsExec, WMI, RDP, WinRM**.

The defenses: **unique** local administrator passwords per machine (**LAPS**), **least privilege** and **tiering** (never use a domain administrator account on a workstation), protecting **LSASS** (**Credential Guard**), disabling NTLM in favor of Kerberos where possible, and monitoring for abnormal logons (EDR/SIEM).

Exam point: **pass-the-hash reuses the NTLM hash without ever knowing the password**. The attacker does not "crack" the hash: they **replay** it as is in order to authenticate. It is an engine of **lateral movement**, and it is especially dangerous when a local admin account shares the same password (and therefore the same hash) across several machines.

cards.back: Value derived from the password, stored and used by Windows for NTLM authentication.
Reuse of a stolen NTLM hash to authenticate without knowing the password.
An attacker's progression from machine to machine inside a compromised network.
Windows process that handles authentication; its memory holds sought-after secrets.
Solution that enforces a unique, managed local administrator password per machine.
